speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 2, interview with Eva Najnudel Kimel, January 23, 1996. I'd like to go back to your-- the period that you spent in Luck and in-- in Ukraine-- with Ukrainian schools and situations of that sort. Was there-- did you a-- any personal experiences in terms of antisemitism or knowledge of-- of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. At that time, we loved Stalin because he was posted as the-- the grandfather [INAUDIBLE] of all the children and whatever. And antisemitist was not allowed supposedly, and it was very hidden. And I don't recall any incidents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your Ukrainian friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were fine. I don't recall any problems of coming home and crying because of that. I haven't any problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your school--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- excuse me-- I think they were afraid because the Russians were holding everybody, you know, really very strict. And I mean, if you denounce somebody, they would have been either in jail, killed, or whatever, or sent to Siberia. Because that's really what was happening to most of the people-- put to jail in Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're in school. What language was used?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any Jewish studies at all for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your Jewish life in your-- within the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's interesting because I remember-- and I was going to go back to that, too. I remember when we were to Luck. Now, as I said, my mother was kosher at home. And the first time she had to eat pork, I remember like today, that she was gagging on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did she have to eat pork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was nothing else to be gotten. I mean, you had to eat what you had to eat, what-- what was available. You had no choice. You had-- you were lucky if you got, you know, food altogether. So that's far as the Jewish life was-- was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- was there a synagogue in Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: If there was-- there probably was. But I don't remember at that time that-- that that was on anybody's mind of the people that were-- that were running away, and they were called the [? Brzeziny. ?] And you know, life was too tough and-- to think about, you know, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work in Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. And your father, what was his, under communist regime, was he in trouble at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he wasn't in trouble, except that, you know, we were afraid that they're going to-- to send them to-- to Siberia. So you had to be-- really have-- you have-- you had to have your ears open and hear what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he was considered a worker, however.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was considered a worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: What contact did you have with your [? mishpachah, ?] with your family, in Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very loose contact--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: --very loose.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what was happening to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think towards the end, I think my mother did receive a few letters, but they were very scarce and very few. And all we knew that they were taken to a ghetto. The ghetto was created, and they were taken to a ghetto. But that was it. We didn't-- we really didn't know. We didn't know about the concentration camps and whatever went on on the western part. We had no idea, I mean, I personally. Maybe my mother did know, but I doubt it because only when we came back to [? Lodz ?] after the war, I started reading all the books that were out already at that time, and I-- I learned about the atrocities that were happening, you know, after-- during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people escape to Luck and-- and to the other towns from the western part at all during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, some of them did. One of my uncles did escape, but that must have been yet before the-- before the ghettos. And he was missing his parents tremendously. He went back and was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And were there newspapers or any kind--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of accounts, any kind of sort of letters, briefs, people come running away? Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people were coming. Yes, people were running away. And I guess they did hear the atrocities, but I-- my recollection, I-- I can't think about it. I-- I don't remember. I don't remember that-- that part.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where did you go from Luck?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Luck, we went to Ludwipol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's right. And where did you live in Ludwipol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, we rented an apartment in some Ukrainian's-- with some Ukrainian's family. And I went to school. My father got a job there. And we led a so-so normal-- I should say normal, quote, 'normal' life, until 1941, where the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you see the Germans first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when-- when they came in, which was not-- not too long after the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your-- that at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. No, I don't remember. All I remember that my uncle, who was with us-- the one, the furrier-- he got the job with the Germans in-- in the German factory or whatever. Because he was making furs, he knew how to-- to sew. At that time, he became just a, you know, furrier, not a businessman. And I guess the Germans-- it was very cold there so they needed fur coats and whatever. So he was working. They gave him a job. He was working in one of the factories, so, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this slave labor or was this paid labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there wasn't paid labor. Maybe they gave them-- maybe they gave them a soup, you know, soup for that. And-- and-- and I remember that they were taking us-- and me, I was what, at that time, maybe 1941, I was 10 years old-- we had to go on the street and pick cigarette butts. And we had to-- to pull grass from in between the stones, like, they had the sidewalk, and there was, like, grass growing in between. So the-- the-- the small children had to go and, you know, pick the thing. And once, I remember, I was taken to a kitchen. I guess it was a-- a German kitchen for-- for the soldiers. And I guess we had to peel potatoes and scrub pots and wash dishes and-- and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess they-- they-- it is possible, now I'm not 100% sure, but it's possible that somebody from the Judenrate, you know, I-- I mean, that the Germans asked the Judenrate to deliver so many children or so many people or whatever. And the Judenrate got-- you know, came to the houses and said, you, you, you, and you, you have to go to work, or you have to come and help or whatever. That's how they were getting us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- how did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, the names were enough for them to know we were Jewish. And besides, I guess we had the-- all the Jews had to register. Now, even-- even during the Russian times, when we were with the Russians, we were given like a-- it's not a passport, but it was like a document that looked almost like a passport. And they ask what-- what was-- where you were born. And if you were born in the western part of Poland, you were called this-- you were under certain paragraph. So they knew if they wanted to-- the Russians-- if they wanted to send you-- to send all the refugees to-- to Russia, they would-- they-- it was just enough to look at that paragraph. And there was the religion stated in the-- in those [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think that we had to register when the Germans came in. I think we had to register. All the Jews probably had to go. When they created that Judenrate, probably all the Jews had to go and register. And then the house we were living in became part of a ghetto. That was probably not too long after the Germans came in. I don't remember how long. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, I remember we had a two-- one bedroom and a living room, yes, and a kitchen. And we were four people-- the four and my two uncles were with us at that time. So we're six people. Well, as far as I remember, all of a sudden, we ended up maybe 20 people in that apartment, because people that lived behind the borders of the ghetto had to come into the ghetto. So we were very cramped and-- [SIGH] and had to give everybody, you know, had to make room for everybody. So people were sleeping on the floors, on the-- wherever there was room. And in the kitchen, you could find 20 people at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never had to move, though. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have to move--

speaker: interviewer
text: You just had to take--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because, yeah, because we were in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the ghetto itself, what did it-- did it have a-- a gate around it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a fence around it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they had a fence around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of-- made out of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: A tough wire, barbed wire, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Barbed wire. And where was the Judenrate in-- within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I remember, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in the Judenrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess people who thought they-- they can help themselves and make life a little bit more comfortable for themselves or bearable went into the Judenrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they picked, or did they volunteer? How did it work?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any names of people in the-- who was the head of the Judenrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember any names.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Were there were Jewish police as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: There probably was, but again, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people in the Judenrate people who were wealthy or learned or-- do you know why they were picked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, no, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the general feeling about the Judenrate? Did people like them or not like them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess there were different people in the Judenrate, too. You know, there were people that tried to help others, and there were people probably that didn't. And again, if you had some connection with somebody there, then-- and-- and they were nice to you, I guess you liked them. And if they were-- but in general, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews lived in Ludwipol? I can't tell you. Was it-- was it a large Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- the whole town was not that big. But I would say it was a sizable community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- were there a lot of people who came from the West to Ludwipol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. But again, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only 10 years old, and I-- I really-- I remember I had friends, and I was playing with them. And-- and I remember that sometimes during the night we would hear sirens and the German saying, Juden raus, Juden raus. And every time that happened, we thought that that was the end of us. These things I do remember. I remember the sirens waking us up in the middle of the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many-- these were [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no they were not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, they would be over. They would just scare the hell out of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take people away?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't. And if they did, maybe there were some certain people, and I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you need to wear any [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. First, we had to wear like a band on our arm. And then-- then we wore a yellow a yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what did it say on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it said Juden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Was your-- what happened with your father? Did he get taken to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents--

speaker: interviewer
text: --slave labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: --while we were in Ludwipol, my parents developed a very close relationship with some Poles that lived not far-- not far away from us. And they thought they were their friends, and maybe they were at that time. I don't know. And we lived in the ghetto for about-- from 19-- I guess-- 41 till probably-- actually, it was 1943-- about probably a year or maybe more. And naturally, there was no school. There was-- there was nothing. I mean, everybody was just-- but one thing I remember. It's interesting. While in the ghetto, I think these Poles-- oh, that's why we thought they were friends-- these Poles or Ukrainians, whoever they were, they gave me a piece of land, you know, a little piece of land. And I remember as a 10-year-old girl, I was tending to it, and I was growing tomatoes and corn and cucumbers and whatever. And that was my daily routine.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the ghetto, right. And that was-- that was my-- my daily routine. And-- and that's why we had cucumbers, and we had some vegetables, because I grew them. Anyhow, my father-- my father was killed during the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] clean-up, or whatever they call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not [NON-ENGLISH], during the-- the [? thing ?] of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they dissolved the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. How-- were you around then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened in-- in the morning, my father went out, I guess to see what's going on. Because that was the thing-- to get up and to go out. And he came running back home and said to my mother, take the children and go to these people-- to these Polish or Ukrainian people-- and stay with them, because I think there's going to be-- it looks like today there's going to be an aktion here. And I'm going to see what's going on, and I'll follow you there and I'll meet you there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my-- my mother took me and my sister, just one light coat-- I remember like today-- a little grey coat. And my sister was born in 1937, so she was-- she was five younger than me. So she was like five years old at that time. And we ran to these people. We somehow got out and ran to these people and knocked at their doors. And naturally, nobody opened the doors for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and at that time already was a lot of tumult there. There was a lot of going on. And we-- she turned around from there and started just going ahead of herself, you know, just not knowing where she was going or what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Towards the ghetto or away from the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, away from the ghetto. And all of a sudden, she saw like a little potato-- what do you call it? It looked like a dog's house, really. And she opened the door from it, and all of a sudden she saw some people in there. And it turned out that it was like a potato-- not a dump, potato--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cellar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a potato cellar. And when she opened it, there were some people already hidden in there. So she pushed me. She pushed my sister in there, and she jumped in there. And while we were there, we found out from the other people, too, that the aktion was going on. And that they-- and we would-- we stayed there in that cellar with our hearts on our shoulders, I have to tell you, because we heard everything that was going on. We heard the Germans passing back and forth. And we heard the squeaking and the screeching and the yelling and the-- and the German language and shooting and-- and all that. And luckily, I don't know what happened, that some other people opened the door. Some other people came in and were saved with us. And we stayed there until the middle of the night. In the middle of the night, thanks to the other people that were there, because my mother wouldn't have any idea where to go or what to do, we came out from there. And we were not far from the Bug, from the River Bug. And in the middle of the night, we crossed that River Bug. And I guess my mother had probably held my sister somehow. Thank god that the river was not really that deep. And me, I don't remember. Somebody probably-- maybe there was a young man there or whatever-- took me on his shoulders and crossed the River Bug. When we crossed the River Bug, we ended up in the forest. And walking in the forest for a couple of hours, all of a sudden, across, my mother saw his-- her brother walking, you know, walking the opposite way. Well, you can imagine, I mean, that reunion, that there was a soul that, you know, family. And that's how that started, hiding in the forest and going through this part of-- later on, while we were in forest and slept, you know, I mean, the-- the first, we slept on the ground and nothing to get changed in, and we were wet from the-- from the water and all that. Then we found out how my father was killed, because somebody escaped from the mass gave. It turned out that there was a mass grave that-- someplace on the outskirts of Ludwipol. And apparently, they must have been taking the-- the Jews from the ghetto to dig these graves. I didn't hear anything about it, but somebody either must have heard, [? though ?] people didn't speak about it because they were afraid to be shot maybe. They-- they lined all these people, you know, at the-- the mass-- mass grave, and the German just shot all of them. Well, this-- this man that was telling us this story was not shot. His daughters next to him were shot. And when he saw that, he-- he jumped. He-- he-- he fell to the grave, too, I mean. And that misled the-- the-- the Germans, because they thought he was dead. In the middle of the night, he escaped-- he escaped the ghetto-- he escaped the-- the graves and whatever. And I think he survived, but I have no idea. I don't remember his name. I don't know where they were-- I-- I know nothing about it, which is a shame.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told us that what had happened with my father, my father went to look for us to these people. Well, he had the same experience as us. So he went back to the apartment, went under the bed, and hid himself under the bed. And he was there. He survived. He survived the cleaning out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at night, I think some Ukrainians or Poles came in to rob the apartment, and they found him there. And they called the Germans, and the Germans took him back to the-- they brought him back to the mass grave, and he was killed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this person who told you the story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. I'd like to go back for a few minutes to the ghetto itself to-- how did people get along there? You were so many in one apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was tough. It was a lot of bickering and a lot of jealousy and a lot of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was tough. It was tough. Now, the people that were in the apartment originally had a little bit more, I guess, power, or they thought they had more power--

speaker: interviewer
text: That means you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --right, than the people that were brought in. But it was very tough because, you know, people were stealing food because the food was so scarce, you know. And so it-- it was-- it was tough. And you know, there you couldn't-- I mean, there were no bathrooms, so we had to go outside and shower outside. And I think there were outhouses. So the hygiene wasn't the greatest.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember once laying in bed, and I didn't feel like getting up next morning. And I said to myself that-- the incidents, you somehow remember some. And I'm laying in bed, I said, oh, I hope I'm sick today. I hope I get sick so I don't have to get up. I don't have to face the world. I don't-- but what do you know? I got up in the morning. I had very high temperature. So I think self-suggestion, you know, worked on you really-- What happened when--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: --somebody was sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there-- I guess there were some doctors that-- you-- you just reminded me of something, [? though. ?] There were some doctors that were attending. And maybe you had some Poles on the outside, and maybe somebody would give you some medication. Or you would-- you could get some medication from-- from the outside world. And if you didn't, you died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- did you get medication? Did a doctor come to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember. Mm-hmm. In-- in terms of relationships in the ghetto, were there any marriages, babies born in-- in-- in that period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I know that there was, but, you know, I was so far away from it. I didn't know about sex at that time yet, you know. I mean, I only learned later on about it. So I-- I really-- I-- I know I heard later on that there-- there were some babies born. As a matter of fact, it's interesting. There was a-- a man that was living with us in the apartment, and he was my father's very good friend. But he had a crush on my mother. And after the war, he wanted to marry my mother, because he survived. But so, I guess there were-- besides, you know, all these problems and these whatever it is, I mean, there was still emotions and relationships and whatever probably formed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you do all day? Did you get a chance to-- to read?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people take care of kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't [? know. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, no. I did take care of kids, but that was-- that was when we were in the forests already.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the ghetto itself--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: --was there any organized--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe--

speaker: interviewer
text: --activities for children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because in some ghettos, there were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, I don't remember any organized. I remember we used to spend time with some friends in each other's house, playing with dolls, playing doctor, playing house, I mean, you know, just regular whatever it is. Then I guess, maybe going outside and playing maybe some ball or-- or whatever. But I don't remember any organized activities. I don't remember reading, [? because I-- ?] I mean, grown-ups maybe yes. Maybe they did have some books or maybe they read. But can't remember anything being done for the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a radio in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I doubt it because I don't think we even had electricity there. You see, this was a very-- like Radom was already, you know, civilized town, a city, whatever. There, I think we used kerosene. It was a really medieval times, [INAUDIBLE] you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess there were-- there must have been Russians. I don't really remember that. Maybe-- maybe my mother didn't look Jewish. Maybe she could go out from the ghetto. Maybe they did let people out for-- I-- I don't remember. But they had to be Russians because, I mean, although the Russians were probably for the-- you know, for-- for the dogs. I know that maybe my uncle was bringing something from the factory. I know that when I was working at the kitchen at that time, I remember they gave me soup. So maybe my father went out to work. Maybe they gave him some. I-- I don't remember what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any money, your family? Did they-- did-- did you have any money put away or that you could bribe or use?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only money I think that we had was-- was that fur.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still--

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe--

speaker: interviewer
text: --had all the fur with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had all the fur with us, until-- yeah. And as a matter of fact, when we were in Ludwipol, I think we didn't have-- we didn't have it in the ghetto. We did-- can't remember. We-- my mother had a big hole dug out, and they put the whole trunk of the fur, you know. And after the war when we went there, my mother wanted to, you know, to find it and take some. It was gone. So somebody found it, and they dug it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you-- did you-- were you able to keep any money with you at all from your-- your trip East? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even--

speaker: interviewer
text: --get the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I don't even know if they had-- you know, I don't even-- that they had money. All they had was-- they were working. My father was working all along. So I think there was enough to sustain ourselves, you know, during the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you ate?

speaker: interviewee
text: --but I don't think-- I don't know whether they had anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food did you eat? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't-- I don't remember. In the ghetto, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. We're going to change our tape now because I want to go on into the woods--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the bunker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I--
